does: PerceptTwin presents a fully automatic pipeline that constructs interactive 3D simulations directly from semantic scene representations captured by a robot's perception system. The approach combines open-vocabulary object maps with neural 3D asset generation, affordance prediction, and commonsense constraint checking to create faithful digital twins of real environments. These reconstructed simulations enable iterative planning and verification: language models generate candidate plans, which are executed in simulation to validate feasibility and safety. The system incorporates an LLM-based judge that verifies plan correctness against human preferences and commonsense constraints. Experiments demonstrate that simulation-based feedback enables LLM planners to iteratively refine plans, improve success rates, enhance safety properties, and resist adversarial prompt injection attacks more effectively than planning without verification.
connects: This work bridges semantic scene understanding, 3D reconstruction, and LLM-based robot planning. It extends recent progress in open-vocabulary object detection and grounding by leveraging semantic maps as foundations for simulation construction. The approach builds on prior work in affordance learning and physics-based simulation for robotics, while incorporating advances in neural 3D asset generation and conditioning mechanisms. The verification framework draws from AI alignment and mechanistic interpretability literature, adapting LLM-based evaluation techniques for robotics contexts. The iterative refinement loop connects to recent trends in prompt engineering and in-context learning where language models benefit from environmental feedback. The work also responds to long-standing challenges in reducing sim-to-real gaps by grounding simulations in actual perceived scenes rather than manual specification.
enables: This pipeline opens new directions for autonomous robot operation in unstructured real-world environments without manual simulation engineering. By automating digital twin creation from perception, it enables rapid deployment to novel environments and scales planning verification to diverse scenarios. The iterative LLM planning loop with simulation feedback suggests pathways toward more robust and aligned robotic systems that can self-correct before execution. Future work could extend this to multi-robot coordination, long-horizon planning with learned temporal dynamics, or integration with more sophisticated physics simulators. The verified planning approach also provides a foundation for scaling to complex manipulation tasks or environments with critical safety constraints. Additionally, the framework could inform broader research in grounding language models in embodied perception and creating feedback loops that improve both plan quality and model reliability without human intervention.
